channelTones.email: Informative and respectful, providing detailed technical information while maintaining warmth
channelTones.crisis: Transparent and solution-focused, maintaining dignity while addressing concerns with competence
channelTones.website: Sophisticated and educational, balancing technical specifications with sensory descriptions and philosophical context
channelTones.socialMedia: Visually driven with thoughtful captions that highlight the craft, process, and architectural applications
channelTones.customerSupport: Professional and knowledgeable, treating each inquiry as a collaboration with fellow design enthusiasts

frameworkData.writingSample: Our firemasters treat each piece of Accoya wood with reverence, using fire not to destroy, but to transform. The ancient Shou Sugi Ban technique creates a deep carbon layer that protects the wood for generations while revealing its natural character. The result is a material of rare beauty—silvery charred surfaces that age gracefully, developing a patina that tells the story of time and weather. This is wood reimagined for modern architecture, yet rooted in 400 years of Japanese tradition.
frameworkData.colorDirection: Deep charcoal blacks, warm natural wood tones, silvery grays, and earthy accents that reflect the charred wood aesthetic and natural origins
frameworkData.imageryDirection: Close-up textures of charred wood showing carbon layer detail, atmospheric shots of firemasters at work with fire, architectural photography showcasing installed projects, natural settings emphasizing FSC origins, before/after transformation imagery

personalityTraits.name: Masterful
personalityTraits.weAreThis: Firemasters who have perfected an ancient art
personalityTraits.description: Expert command of the Shou Sugi Ban technique with deep respect for traditional craftsmanship

personalityTraits.name: Refined
personalityTraits.weAreThis: Connoisseurs of natural beauty and architectural elegance
personalityTraits.description: Appreciation for subtle beauty, natural patina, and the Wabi Sabi philosophy of imperfect perfection

personalityTraits.name: Purposeful

personalityTraits.name: Passionate
personalityTraits.weAreThis: Artisans who live and breathe our craft
personalityTraits.description: Genuine love for wood, fire, and the transformation process

frameworkData.typographyDirection: Clean, modern serif or refined sans-serif that balances traditional craftsmanship with contemporary architecture—legible, elegant, timeless
frameworkData.brandVoiceDescription: Zwarthout speaks with the quiet confidence of a master craftsman—knowledgeable without being condescending, passionate without being overly emotional, and sophisticated without being pretentious. The voice honors tradition while embracing innovation, using descriptive language that appeals to the senses and the intellect.

brandAsset.name: Brand Story
brandAsset.description: Your brand's past, present and future

brandThemes: Wabi Sabi—beauty in imperfection and aging
frameworkData.originStory: Founded in 2012 in Lunteren, The Netherlands, Zwarthout was born from a passion for wood and fire, and a fascination with the ancient Japanese Shou Sugi Ban technique. The founders recognized that this 400-year-old method of charring wood could solve modern architectural challenges—creating beautiful, sustainable, and maintenance-free materials for contemporary buildings.
proofPoints: Fire class B certification for Yoroi without chemical treatments
Operating since 2012 with growing architectural portfolio
frameworkData.abtStatement: Architects and premium homeowners want natural wood for its beauty AND need materials that endure without maintenance, BUT conventional wood fades and rots while synthetic alternatives lack soul, THEREFORE Zwarthout transforms wood through fire using the ancient Shou Sugi Ban technique, creating charred materials that last 50+ years and grow more beautiful with time.
frameworkData.worldContext: In a world of disposable materials and synthetic alternatives, where buildings age poorly and require constant maintenance, where sustainability is often compromised for convenience, architects and homeowners struggle to find materials that are both beautiful and enduring.
frameworkData.elevatorPitch: Zwarthout transforms wood through fire using ancient Japanese techniques, creating maintenance-free architectural materials that last 50+ years.
frameworkData.manifestoText:
We are firemasters. We honor a 400-year-old tradition that understood what modern building has forgotten: fire doesn't destroy—it transforms. It protects. It reveals the soul of wood.

We believe buildings should age like fine wine, not spoiled milk. That sustainability isn't a compromise—it's a commitment to materials that endure for generations. That true beauty embraces imperfection, developing character and patina over time.

We char wood from sustainable forests, creating a carbon layer that shields it from the elements for 50 years or more. No toxic chemicals. No endless maintenance. Just wood, fire, and craftsmanship.

This is Shou Sugi Ban. This is Wabi Sabi. This is architecture that honors both tradition and tomorrow.

This is Zwarthout.
valuesInAction: Sustainability: Using only FSC-certified wood and creating products with 50+ year lifespans
Craftsmanship: Training firemasters in the traditional Shou Sugi Ban technique and offering professional mounting services to preserve the carbon layer
brandMilestones: 2012: Founded in Lunteren with passion for Shou Sugi Ban
Development of Marugame product line with Accoya wood
Launch of Yoroi bamboo product for fire class B requirements
Expansion of firemaster team and mounting services
frameworkData.empathyStatement: We understand the architect's dilemma—you envision timeless beauty, but conventional materials force you to compromise. You want natural wood, but dread the maintenance and deterioration.
frameworkData.founderMotivation: A deep respect for traditional craftsmanship combined with a vision to bring sustainable, enduring beauty to modern architecture through the transformative power of fire
frameworkData.coreBeliefStatement: We believe that the most beautiful and sustainable materials are created when ancient wisdom meets modern innovation, when fire transforms wood into something that endures for generations.
frameworkData.authorityCredentials: Since 2012, our firemasters have perfected the Shou Sugi Ban technique, creating charred wood that lasts 50+ years. Our Marugame product holds Cradle-to-Cradle Gold certification. We work with premium FSC-certified Accoya and bamboo, and our Yoroi product meets fire class B requirements naturally, without chemicals.
keyNarrativeMessages: Fire transforms wood into something more enduring than it was before
400 years of Japanese tradition meets contemporary Dutch architecture
True sustainability means creating materials that last generations
The carbon layer is both protection and beauty
Our firemasters are artisans, not manufacturers
frameworkData.philosophicalProblem: The modern building industry has lost connection with materials that have soul and story, replacing craftsmanship with mass production and natural beauty with synthetic imitations
frameworkData.stakesCostOfInaction: Buildings clad in conventional wood that deteriorate within years, requiring costly replacement or toxic chemical treatments. Architectural visions compromised by material limitations. The loss of traditional craftsmanship and sustainable building practices.
frameworkData.customerSuccessVision: Architects creating award-winning buildings with distinctive black facades that become landmarks. Homeowners enjoying maintenance-free exteriors that develop a graceful patina over decades. Buildings that honor both tradition and innovation, standing as testaments to sustainable design.
frameworkData.transformationPromise: Transform your architectural vision into enduring reality with charred wood that grows more beautiful with age, requires zero maintenance, and stands for generations
frameworkData.customerExternalProblem: Finding exterior wood materials that won't fade, rot, or require extensive maintenance, while meeting strict fire safety regulations without toxic chemical treatments
frameworkData.customerInternalProblem: The frustration of compromising between aesthetic vision and practical durability; the desire to create buildings that age gracefully rather than deteriorate

brandAsset.name: Core Values
brandAsset.description: Fundamental beliefs that guide your brand

ownValue.name: Transformation
ownValue.description: The belief that fire transforms wood into something more beautiful and enduring than it was before. This is Zwarthout's unique philosophy—seeing potential where others see limitation, using destruction as a creative force, and embracing the Wabi Sabi principle that imperfection and aging create deeper beauty.

anchorValue1.name: Craftsmanship
anchorValue1.description: Deep commitment to mastering the Shou Sugi Ban technique and treating each piece of wood with artisanal care. This is the foundation of everything Zwarthout does—the firemasters, the process, the attention to detail.

anchorValue2.name: Sustainability
anchorValue2.description: Genuine environmental responsibility through FSC-certified materials, 50+ year product lifespans, and Cradle-to-Cradle certification. Not greenwashing, but fundamental commitment to materials that endure.

frameworkData.valueTension: The tension between honoring traditional craftsmanship (which is slow, artisanal, and limited in scale) and the aspiration to innovate and partner with more architects on larger projects (which requires efficiency and scalability). Zwarthout must grow without compromising the firemaster approach that makes them unique.

aspirationValue1.name: Innovation
aspirationValue1.description: Continuously evolving the ancient technique to meet modern architectural needs—developing new products like Yoroi for fire safety, exploring new applications, and pushing the boundaries of what charred wood can achieve.

aspirationValue2.name: Architectural Partnership
aspirationValue2.description: Moving beyond supplier to become a trusted collaborator with architects and designers, co-creating distinctive buildings that become landmarks and inspire the industry.

brandAsset.name: Purpose Statement
brandAsset.description: The reason your organization exists beyond profit

frameworkData.mechanism: Through the traditional Shou Sugi Ban technique, firemasters transform sustainable wood with fire, creating a protective carbon layer that ensures 50+ year durability while honoring Wabi Sabi philosophy and natural beauty.
frameworkData.statement: To honor the ancient art of transforming wood through fire, creating enduring beauty that connects modern architecture with timeless craftsmanship and natural sustainability.
frameworkData.pressureTest: Does this purpose remain true if Zwarthout scaled globally? Yes - the commitment to fire-charring craftsmanship and sustainable transformation is scalable. Does it differentiate from commodity wood suppliers? Yes - the fusion of ancient technique with modern sustainability is distinctive.
frameworkData.impactDescription: Zwarthout enables architects, designers, and homeowners to realize their vision for distinctive, sustainable spaces by providing exclusive charred wood that combines ancient Japanese tradition with modern performance standards.

brandAsset.name: Social Relevancy
brandAsset.description: Your brand's societal and environmental impact

frameworkData.impactNarrative: Zwarthout addresses the building industry's sustainability crisis by offering an alternative to both short-lived conventional wood and synthetic materials. By using FSC-certified wood and creating products with 50+ year lifespans, they reduce resource consumption and waste. The Cradle-to-Cradle Gold certification demonstrates commitment to circular economy principles. The natural fire resistance of Yoroi eliminates the need for toxic chemical treatments.
frameworkData.impactStatement: Creating architectural materials that last 50+ years, sourced from responsibly managed forests, reducing the building industry's environmental footprint through enduring beauty.
keyStakeholders: Architects and designers seeking sustainable materials
Sustainable forestry partners in New Zealand and China
Premium homeowners committed to environmental responsibility
frameworkData.annualCommitment: Maintain 100% FSC-certified wood sourcing, pursue Cradle-to-Cradle certification for all product lines, and develop materials that extend industry-standard lifespans
activationChannels: Product certifications and transparency about sourcing
Educational content about product lifespan vs. conventional materials
Case studies showing long-term performance and reduced replacement needs
Thought leadership on circular economy in building materials
communicationPrinciples: Lead with product longevity and lifecycle impact, not just material sourcing
Demonstrate sustainability through certifications and measurable commitments
Connect traditional techniques to modern environmental solutions
Educate about the environmental cost of disposable building materials
Show how beauty and sustainability are inseparable, not trade-offs

goals.title: Establish Shou Sugi Ban as the Premium Standard for Sustainable Wood Architecture
goals.description: Make charred wood the first choice for architects designing sustainable, distinctive buildings across Europe and beyond
goals.theoryOfChange: By proving that charred wood outperforms conventional materials in longevity, aesthetics, and sustainability, architects will increasingly specify Shou Sugi Ban for premium projects. As these buildings become landmarks, the technique gains recognition, creating demand that shifts industry standards toward materials designed for generational endurance.
goals.measurableCommitment: Achieve 50+ landmark architectural projects featuring Zwarthout products; establish presence in 10+ European countries; demonstrate measurable reduction in building material replacement cycles

goals.title: Preserve and Evolve the Firemaster Craft for Future Generations
goals.description: Create a training academy and certification program that ensures the Shou Sugi Ban technique is passed to new artisans while continuing to innovate applications
goals.theoryOfChange: Traditional crafts disappear when knowledge isn't transferred. By creating formal training and certification, Zwarthout ensures the technique survives and evolves. Certified firemasters become ambassadors for the craft, expanding its application while maintaining quality standards. This preserves cultural heritage while driving innovation.
goals.measurableCommitment: Train and certify 20+ new firemasters; develop apprenticeship program; document and codify the technique for knowledge preservation; launch at least 2 new product innovations

frameworkData.mtpNarrative: Zwarthout envisions a future where buildings are designed for generations, not decades. Where wood is valued not for its cheapness but for its character. Where ancient techniques inform modern sustainability. By mastering fire as a creative force, Zwarthout is redefining what wood can be in contemporary architecture.

brandIntegration.positioningLink: The transformative goals reinforce Zwarthout's Creator archetype positioning—they're not just selling products, they're transforming an industry and preserving a craft. The MTP elevates them from material supplier to industry change agent.
campaignDirections: Industry thought leadership on material longevity and sustainability
brandIntegration.internalActivation: Firemaster training programs, innovation workshops exploring new applications, sustainability metrics tracking, and storytelling that connects daily work to the larger mission of transforming the industry
communicationThemes: The firemaster as artisan and innovator
Charred wood as the future of sustainable facades

frameworkData.massiveTransformativePurpose: To transform the building industry's relationship with wood—from disposable commodity to enduring architectural art.

brandAsset.name: Golden Circle
brandAsset.description: Simon Sinek's WHY → HOW → WHAT framework

how.details: Our firemasters use the traditional Japanese charring technique, combining old-fashioned craftsmanship with modern technology. We carefully select FSC-certified Accoya wood and bamboo, then char them to create a protective carbon layer. This process requires expertise to achieve the perfect balance - deep enough for durability, refined enough for architectural beauty.
how.statement: We transform sustainable wood through the controlled art of fire, guided by master craftsmen who balance tradition with innovation.

why.details: Zwarthout exists because we have a deep passion for wood and fire, and reverence for the 300-year-old Japanese Shou Sugi Ban tradition. We believe that by honoring this ancient craft, we can create materials that embody Wabi Sabi philosophy - finding beauty in imperfection and impermanence - while delivering modern performance and sustainability.
why.statement: We believe that the most enduring materials are born from the marriage of ancient wisdom and natural elements.

what.details: Our product lines include Marugame (charred Accoya with silvery finish and Cradle-to-Cradle Gold certification) and Yoroi (deeply charred bamboo with natural fire resistance). We also provide professional mounting services to ensure the carbon layer remains intact during installation.
what.statement: We produce exclusive charred wood products for facades and interiors that last 50+ years with zero maintenance.

brandAsset.name: Brand Essence
brandAsset.description: The heart and soul of your brand

proofPoints: Cradle-to-Cradle Gold certification for Marugame product line
50+ year guaranteed lifespan with zero maintenance requirements
Fire class B-s1, d0 certification for Yoroi bamboo without chemical additives
Specialized firemaster craftsmen trained in 300-year-old Japanese technique

frameworkData.audienceInsight: Architects and premium homeowners seek materials that transcend mere function - they want their spaces to embody authenticity, sustainability, and timeless beauty without the burden of ongoing maintenance or environmental compromise.
frameworkData.emotionalBenefit: Pride in owning exclusive, artisanally-crafted materials that connect your space to centuries of Japanese tradition and Wabi Sabi philosophy
frameworkData.essenceNarrative: Zwarthout embodies the transformative power of fire meeting wood, where ancient Japanese mastery creates modern architectural distinction. Through the Shou Sugi Ban tradition, we forge materials that honor imperfection, celebrate natural patina, and endure for generations without compromise.
frameworkData.functionalBenefit: Maintenance-free charred wood that lasts 50+ years with natural fire resistance and weather protection through a hardened carbon layer
frameworkData.selfExpressiveBenefit: Express your commitment to authentic craftsmanship, environmental consciousness, and architectural distinction through materials that tell a story of fire and transformation

proofPoints: Fire class B-s1, d0 without chemical additives
Professional mounting service to protect carbon layer integrity
frameworkData.differentiator: Traditional Japanese Shou Sugi Ban fire-charring technique combined with Cradle-to-Cradle Gold certification and FSC-certified sustainable sourcing
frameworkData.emotionalValue: Confidence and pride in specifying or owning exclusive materials crafted by firemasters using 300-year-old tradition, connecting your project to authentic Japanese craftsmanship
frameworkData.targetAudience: Architects, interior designers, and premium homeowners who value distinctive aesthetics, sustainability, and long-term performance in building materials
frameworkData.functionalValue: Charred wood products with 50+ year lifespan, zero maintenance requirements, natural fire resistance, and weather protection through hardened carbon layer
frameworkData.promiseOneLiner: Timeless beauty, forged by fire, sustained by nature.
frameworkData.coreCustomerNeed: Exclusive, sustainable wood materials that deliver architectural distinction and enduring performance without maintenance burden or environmental compromise
frameworkData.promiseStatement: Zwarthout promises to deliver architecturally distinctive, maintenance-free wood that endures for 50+ years, crafted through ancient Japanese fire-charring tradition and certified sustainable practices.
frameworkData.onlynessStatement: Only Zwarthout can deliver maintenance-free charred wood with 50+ year durability because we combine authentic Japanese Shou Sugi Ban firemaster craftsmanship with Cradle-to-Cradle Gold certified sustainable materials.
measurableOutcomes: Zero maintenance required over 50+ year product lifespan
Natural fire resistance meeting B-s1, d0 classification
Carbon layer durability through proper installation
frameworkData.selfExpressiveValue: Demonstrate your sophisticated taste, environmental values, and appreciation for artisanal quality through materials that embody Wabi Sabi philosophy and sustainable innovation

brandAsset.name: Mission & Vision
brandAsset.description: What you do today and where you're headed

frameworkData.forWhom: Architects, interior designers, and discerning homeowners who seek distinctive, sustainable materials for modern buildings and luxury spaces
frameworkData.whatWeDo: Produce exclusive charred wood products using traditional Japanese Shou Sugi Ban fire-charring technique, combining FSC-certified sustainable materials with firemaster craftsmanship
frameworkData.howWeDoIt: Through the careful balance of ancient tradition and modern technology, our firemasters char sustainable Accoya wood and bamboo to create protective carbon layers that ensure 50+ year durability while honoring Wabi Sabi philosophy
frameworkData.impactGoal: Demonstrate that ancient wisdom and natural processes can outperform chemical treatments, proving that sustainability and superior performance are not trade-offs but complementary outcomes
frameworkData.boldAspiration: Establish Shou Sugi Ban charred wood as the premium standard for sustainable architectural cladding across Europe and beyond, replacing chemical-treated alternatives
frameworkData.missionOneLiner: Mastering fire and wood to forge enduring architectural beauty.
frameworkData.valuesAlignment: Passion for traditional craftsmanship, respect for natural materials, commitment to environmental sustainability, dedication to enduring quality over short-term convenience
frameworkData.visionStatement: To be recognized globally as the definitive authority in Shou Sugi Ban craftsmanship, where every architect and designer seeking enduring, sustainable wood materials thinks first of Zwarthout's fire-forged excellence.
frameworkData.missionStatement: To transform sustainable wood through the ancient art of Shou Sugi Ban, creating exclusive charred products that unite Japanese craftsmanship tradition with modern architectural performance and environmental responsibility.
successIndicators: Shou Sugi Ban specified in 50%+ of premium sustainable building projects in target markets
Recognition as the European center of excellence for traditional Japanese charring technique
Expansion of Cradle-to-Cradle Gold certification across entire product portfolio
Training and certification program for next generation of firemasters established
Carbon-negative lifecycle achievement across all products
frameworkData.desiredFutureState: A world where sustainable architecture naturally incorporates fire-charred wood, where the Zwarthout name is synonymous with Japanese charring mastery, and where our Cradle-to-Cradle certified materials set the environmental benchmark for the building industry
frameworkData.stakeholderBenefit: Architects gain access to distinctive sustainable materials, homeowners enjoy maintenance-free beauty for generations, the environment benefits from circular economy practices, and the ancient craft of Shou Sugi Ban is preserved and elevated
frameworkData.missionVisionTension: The mission focuses on mastering and honoring the traditional Shou Sugi Ban craft today, while the vision pushes toward mainstream adoption and scale - requiring balance between artisanal exclusivity and broader accessibility without compromising craftsmanship standards

frameworkData.coreFear: Mediocrity, mass production, loss of traditional craftsmanship
frameworkData.strategy: Develop distinctive competence and skill through the mastery of ancient Japanese techniques combined with modern innovation
frameworkData.brandGoal: To transform ordinary wood into extraordinary architectural elements through fire and mastery
frameworkData.coreDesire: To create enduring beauty and value through masterful transformation of natural materials
frameworkData.giftTalent: The ability to harness fire as a creative force, transforming wood into timeless, sustainable architectural beauty
frameworkData.shadowWeakness: Perfectionism that may slow production; potential elitism that could limit market reach
frameworkData.contentStrategy: Behind-the-scenes content showing firemasters at work, educational content about Shou Sugi Ban tradition, project showcases highlighting architectural applications, sustainability narratives, and the philosophy of Wabi Sabi
frameworkData.archetypeInAction: Zwarthout embodies the Creator through their firemasters who practice the ancient Shou Sugi Ban technique, treating each piece of wood as a canvas for transformation. They don't just sell building materials—they craft exclusive architectural elements that blend 400-year-old Japanese tradition with contemporary design.
frameworkData.customerExperience: Clients are invited into a world of craftsmanship where they collaborate with artisans to create something unique. The mounting service ensures the artistic integrity is preserved during installation.
frameworkData.marketingExpression: Emphasis on the artisanal process, the transformation through fire, the uniqueness of each piece, and the marriage of tradition with innovation. Visual storytelling showcasing the charring process and the distinctive carbon layer texture.
frameworkData.competitiveLandscape: Zwarthout operates in a niche where traditional wood suppliers offer commodity materials and modern composite manufacturers offer synthetic alternatives. Zwarthout transcends both by positioning charred wood as an artisanal architectural element—neither commodity nor synthetic, but a crafted material with soul, story, and sustainability.
frameworkData.storytellingApproach: Origin stories of the technique, transformation narratives (wood to charred beauty), craftsman profiles, project case studies showing before/after, and the journey from New Zealand forests to Dutch architecture

contentGuidelines: OBSERVED: Lead with craftsmanship and tradition — reference the ancient Japanese Shou Sugi Ban technique and the brand's 13-year heritage in nearly every product or about-us context.
OBSERVED: Emphasize sustainability and natural materials — use FSC certification, wood origin, and environmental benefits (no chemicals, biodegradable, cradle-to-cradle) as core selling points.
OBSERVED: Highlight uniqueness and variation — remind readers that each plank is hand-charred and differs from the next, reinforcing exclusivity and artisanal quality.
RECOMMENDED: Use project case studies and customer testimonials to demonstrate real-world applications and build trust, especially for B2B and architect audiences.
RECOMMENDED: Provide technical transparency — include fire class ratings, durability class, lifespan estimates, and maintenance requirements in product descriptions to serve specifiers and builders.
writingGuidelines: OBSERVED: Use medium-length sentences (15–25 words) with clear, descriptive language — avoid overly complex syntax but maintain a professional, knowledgeable tone.
OBSERVED: Favor active voice and first-person plural ('we char', 'our charring masters', 'we use') to convey ownership and pride in the craft.
OBSERVED: Integrate technical specifications naturally within narrative copy — fire class, wood type, origin, and application are woven into product stories rather than isolated in tables.
OBSERVED: Embrace poetic, evocative language when describing aesthetics — 'silvery glow', 'refined markings', 'beautiful patina', 'craquelé surface' — to elevate the material beyond commodity.
RECOMMENDED: Use rhetorical questions sparingly to engage readers (e.g., 'Does the wood give off?') — reserve for FAQ or educational content, not product pages.
RECOMMENDED: Close product or section copy with a clear, low-pressure call to action ('Order a sample box', 'Request quote') to guide next steps without hard selling.
brandVoiceguide.voiceSample: Since 2012 Zwarthout | Shou Sugi Ban produces exclusive wood products by treating the wood with fire. Our charring masters char each wooden board in our own furnace, resulting in a unique, sustainable carbon layer. The special Shou Sugi Ban charring technique we use is inspired by an age-old Japanese tradition. Our attention to detail, our passion for wood and sustainability are all reflected in each one of our products, which all have their own appearance.

examplePhrases.text: Our charring masters char each wooden board in our own furnace, resulting in a unique, sustainable carbon layer.
The silvery glow of the charred layer has a different look depending on the angle of the sunlight.
That way, you can experience the attention, care and love we pour into each piece of wood yourself.
Revolutionize your space with cutting-edge materials!
Limited-time offer — buy now before it's too late!
Unlock the secret to stunning facades.

brandStyleguide.designPhilosophy: Industrial craftsmanship meets digital precision: dark surfaces, sharp 0.375rem corners, vibrant orange CTAs, and charred-wood photography create a premium, architectural aesthetic for fire-treated wood products.

headlines: Characteristic, versatile and a silvery charred layer
Deeply charred bamboo, natural appearance, refined markings and fire class B
testimonialQuotes: At ORGA, we're big fans of biobased materials that truly prove their worth. Less maintenance, and a building that only becomes more beautiful with age. That, to us, is what truly future-proof design looks like.

We chose Yoroi for several reasons. First of all, we like the Japanese classical technique used for burning the bamboo. Due to the burning technique Shou Sugi Ban, Yoroi has a fire class B and is maintenance-free.

brandStyleguide.archetypeReasoning: Zwarthout's brand-kern draait volledig om transformatie door vakmanschap — 'firemasters' die hout door vuur omvormen tot architectonische kunst. De waarden (craftsmanship, innovation, transformation), het purpose ('honor the ancient art of transforming wood through fire'), en de hele narratief ('we don't just sell materials—we craft exclusive architectural elements') wijzen unaniem naar Creator. De angst voor mediocrity en mass production, plus de focus op 'enduring beauty through masterful transformation', zijn textbook Creator-signalen. Geen andere archetype past bij deze combinatie van artisanale creatie, innovatie en trots op vakmanschap.
logoGuidelines: Primary logo features 'zwart hout' wordmark in burnt orange (#E06000) with 'Shou Sugi Ban' tagline beneath
Logo uses a distinctive geometric, angular letterform style that evokes wood grain and craftsmanship
Maintain clear space around logo equal to the height of the 'z' character
Logo works best on dark backgrounds (black, charcoal) or light neutral backgrounds
Preserve the two-line lockup: brand name above, tagline below
Use the full-color version (#E06000) as primary; ensure sufficient contrast with background
logoDonts: Don't separate the wordmark from the 'Shou Sugi Ban' tagline
Don't change the burnt orange brand color to other hues
Don't rotate, skew, or distort the angular letterforms
Don't place on busy photographic backgrounds that reduce legibility
Don't recreate or substitute the custom letterforms with standard fonts
Don't use the logo smaller than 120px width to maintain legibility of the tagline
colorDonts: Don't use the burnt orange (#E06000) as a large background color — reserve it for accents, CTAs, and highlights
Don't combine multiple Bootstrap semantic colors (success green, warning yellow, danger red) in the same view unless indicating system states
Don't use low-contrast pairings — ensure burnt orange has sufficient contrast when used for text or interactive elements
Don't replace the primary burnt orange with the Bootstrap blue (#0D6EFD) for brand CTAs
Don't use pure black (#000000) — prefer the charcoal (#212529) for better readability and warmth

photographyStyle.mood: RECOMMENDED: Contemplative, refined, and tactile — imagery should evoke craftsmanship, natural beauty, and timeless quality. Use natural light to highlight texture and the interplay of shadow on charred surfaces.
photographyStyle.subjects: RECOMMENDED: Close-ups of charred wood grain and silvery carbon layers; wide shots of completed facades in natural settings (rural, forested, or minimalist modern contexts); hands of craftspeople at work in the furnace; architectural projects showcasing contrast between black wood and natural surroundings.
photographyStyle.composition: RECOMMENDED: Use strong geometric lines and symmetry to reflect precision and modern architecture; incorporate negative space to convey calm and Japanese aesthetic principles (wabi-sabi); shoot in golden hour or overcast light to emphasize texture without harsh glare.

photographyGuidelines: RECOMMENDED: Feature macro shots of wood texture — show the carbon layer, grain, and natural variation to communicate uniqueness and quality.
RECOMMENDED: Photograph completed projects in situ — capture facades in real environments (forests, fields, urban settings) to demonstrate versatility and integration with nature.
RECOMMENDED: Include process imagery — show charring masters at work, wood in the furnace, or hands finishing a plank to reinforce artisanal credibility.
RECOMMENDED: Use muted, natural color palettes — let the black wood be the hero; surround it with earth tones, greens, grays, and warm wood interiors.
RECOMMENDED: Avoid overly staged or sterile product shots — prefer authentic, lived-in spaces and real construction sites to align with the brand's honest, craft-focused tone.
illustrationGuidelines: RECOMMENDED: Use minimal line drawings or diagrams to explain technical details (e.g., rail system installation, fire class ratings, wood cross-sections) — keep style clean and architectural.
RECOMMENDED: Incorporate subtle Japanese-inspired graphic elements (e.g., simple brush strokes, geometric patterns) in supporting brand materials to reinforce the Shou Sugi Ban heritage.
RECOMMENDED: Avoid decorative or playful illustration styles — this brand is rooted in tradition, craftsmanship, and technical precision, not whimsy.
imageryDonts: Avoid stock photos of generic construction or anonymous workers — imagery must feel specific to Zwarthout and the Shou Sugi Ban process.
Avoid overly bright, high-contrast, or saturated imagery — the brand aesthetic is natural, muted, and refined.
Avoid imagery that hides the wood texture or carbon layer — the material itself is the hero and must be visible and tactile.
Avoid urban-only or hyper-modern contexts that ignore the brand's connection to nature and tradition.
Avoid imagery that suggests mass production or industrial scale — every plank is hand-charred, and visuals should reflect artisanal care.

graphicElements.usageNotes: Design system uses Bootstrap 5 foundation with custom orange brand accent. Minimal rounding (0.375rem standard) creates a modern, structured feel. Gradient overlays add visual interest to hero sections while maintaining readability.
visualDevices: Cookie consent modal with tab navigation (Consent/Details/About)
High-contrast CTA buttons (orange #E06000 on dark backgrounds)
Icon+text pairings in navigation and buttons

graphicElementsDonts: Avoid heavy border-radius values that soften the brand's structured, architectural aesthetic
Don't use gradients on small UI elements like buttons or badges — reserve for hero sections and large backgrounds
Avoid mixing warm orange accents with cool gradients in the same visual block

patterns: Solid color blocks with gradient overlays for hero sections
textures: Wood grain photography as background imagery (brand-relevant texture)
patternsTextures.usageNotes: Brand leverages dark backgrounds to create premium feel and make orange CTAs pop. Gradients are vibrant and saturated, used sparingly for impact. Wood textures in photography reinforce the Shou Sugi Ban (charred wood) product focus.
backgrounds: Light neutral (#F8F9FA) for secondary content areas

iconographyStyle.colorUsage: Orange (#E06000) for active/interactive icons, white (#fff) on dark backgrounds, gray (#6C757D) for inactive states
iconographyStyle.usageNotes: Icons use minimal stroke weight (1px median from borders) with sharp corners (0px median border-radius). Bootstrap icon system detected in SVG data URIs. Icons appear in navigation, buttons (cube icon for 'Order a sample box', document icon for 'Request quote'), and form controls.

iconographyDonts: Don't use filled icons — maintain line-based consistency throughout
Avoid rounded icon corners that conflict with the sharp, architectural brand aesthetic
Don't use icon colors outside the defined palette (orange, white, grays)

layoutPrinciples.usageNotes: Bootstrap 5 grid foundation with 8px base spacing unit. Layout emphasizes horizontal rhythm through consistent 24px spacing. Dark UI surfaces use 10px offset shadows for card elevation.
compositionRules: Modal dialogs use 0.375rem border-radius with 1px borders
Buttons maintain 0.375rem corner radius with transparent borders
Shadows are subtle (0.5rem 1rem rgba) or bold (10px 5px 5px #262626) for depth hierarchy
layoutPrinciples.whitespacePhilosophy: Generous vertical spacing (median 24px) creates breathing room between content blocks. Dark backgrounds amplify whitespace perception.

visualLanguage.summary: Zwarthout employs a minimalist, geometric visual language with sharp rectangular forms and subtle 4px corner radii. The design emphasizes spacious layouts with generous whitespace, using dark surfaces (#212529) elevated by pronounced offset shadows (10px 5px) to create depth. The warm PRIMARY orange (#E06000) accent punctuates an otherwise neutral gray palette, applied sparingly to CTAs and interactive elements.

components.cardStyle: Rectangular cards with minimal 4px corner radius. Dark cards (#212529) use 10px horizontal and 5px vertical shadow offset with #262626 shadow color for pronounced elevation. White cards use lighter 0.5rem 1rem rgba(33,37,41,0.15) shadows.
components.inputStyle: Sharp rectangular forms with thin 1px borders in neutral gray. Minimal corner radius (0-4px). Clean, functional appearance with no decorative elements.
components.buttonShape: Rectangular with 4px corner radius. Solid color fills, no outlines. Sen Bold typography. Minimal padding following 8px spacing system.
components.spacingSystem: Bootstrap 5 grid with 4px base unit. Horizontal rhythm emphasizes 24px spacing between major elements. Generous section padding creates breathing room. 8px increments for component-level spacing.

visualLanguage.promptFragment: Use sharp, geometric rectangular shapes with minimal 4px corner radius on all containers and cards. Apply dark surfaces using #212529 with pronounced shadows: 10px horizontal offset, 5px vertical offset, #262626 shadow color for elevated cards. Use white or #F8F9FA backgrounds for alternating sections. Buttons are rectangular (4px radius) with solid #E06000 fill for primary actions, #212529 for secondary. Typography uses Sen Bold for headings, Roboto for body. Maintain spacious layouts with 24px horizontal spacing between major elements, 8px base unit for component spacing. Thin 1px borders in neutral gray for inputs and dividers. No gradients on primary surfaces. Accent color #E06000 used only for CTAs, links, and active states—never for large background areas. Strict grid alignment, no organic shapes or decorative elements. Minimal text density with generous whitespace ratio. Shadows are subtle on light backgrounds (0 0.5rem 1rem rgba(33,37,41,0.15)), pronounced on dark surfaces. No glassmorphism, no rounded pills, no bold shadows. Clean, functional, craftsmanship-focused aesthetic.

colorApplication.accentUsage: PRIMARY #E06000 used sparingly for CTAs, active states, and key interactive elements. Never used for large background areas. Accent color provides warmth against neutral gray palette.
colorApplication.buttonStyle: Solid fills using PRIMARY #E06000 for primary actions, NEUTRAL #212529 for secondary actions. Minimal hover states with subtle opacity shifts.
colorApplication.backgroundApproach: Clean white and NEUTRAL #F8F9FA alternating sections. Dark UI surfaces use NEUTRAL #212529 with elevated cards creating contrast through shadow rather than background color variation.

styleguideColor.name: Burnt Orange
styleguideColor.notes: Primary brand color extracted from logo. Appears on CTA buttons ('Request quote', 'Allow all') and as accent color throughout the interface. Strong brand identifier.

styleguideColor.name: Off White
styleguideColor.notes: Bootstrap light neutral. Used for light backgrounds and surface areas. Not prominently visible in screenshots but present in framework styles.

styleguideColor.name: Charcoal Black
styleguideColor.notes: Bootstrap dark neutral. Strong usage — appears as primary text color and dark backgrounds throughout the interface, including the cookie consent modal.

styleguideLogo.description: Orange and black 'zwart hout' wordmark with 'Shou Sugi Ban' tagline underneath

persona.name: Sophie Janssen, The Luxury Homeowner
persona.location: Affluent suburbs of Amsterdam, Utrecht, or countryside estates
persona.occupation: Business owner, senior executive, or successful entrepreneur
persona.education: University degree in business or creative field
persona.personalityType: Sophie has refined taste and appreciates quality craftsmanship. She's environmentally conscious and willing to invest in premium materials that align with her values. She views her home as a reflection of her success and personal philosophy, seeking unique elements that can't be found in typical luxury developments.
coreValues: Quality and exclusivity over mass-market options
goals: Invest in materials that maintain beauty without constant upkeep
Increase property value through unique features
motivations: Desire for a home that reflects personal values and taste
Pride in owning something exclusive and handcrafted
Avoiding the maintenance headaches of traditional wood
Creating a peaceful, natural living environment
Making sustainable choices that don't compromise on luxury
frustrations: Wood facades that fade to gray or require annual treatment
Generic luxury finishes that look like everyone else's
High-maintenance materials that demand constant attention
Difficulty finding truly sustainable premium materials
Contractors who don't understand specialized materials
behaviors: Works closely with architects and interior designers
Visits showrooms and requests physical samples
Invests in premium solutions with long-term value
preferredChannels: Instagram and Pinterest for design inspiration
Exclusive home tours and design events
persona.quote: I want my home to be beautiful, sustainable, and effortless. I'm done with materials that look stunning for two years and then become a maintenance burden.
persona.bio: Sophie is building her dream countryside estate after years of success in the corporate world. She's deeply involved in every design decision, working with a renowned architect to create a home that balances modern luxury with natural materials. She's particularly drawn to Japanese design principles and seeks materials that embody both elegance and environmental responsibility.
buyingTriggers: New build or major renovation project
Dissatisfaction with fading or deteriorating wood
Desire to create a unique architectural statement
Recommendation from trusted architect or designer
Seeing charred wood in aspirational design publications

persona.name: Jan de Vries, The Interior Design Specialist
persona.location: Major Dutch cities or design hubs
persona.education: Degree in Interior Design or Architecture
persona.personalityType: Jan is creative and trend-aware, always seeking materials that give his projects a distinctive edge. He balances aesthetic vision with practical considerations like fire safety and maintenance. He prides himself on discovering unique materials before they become mainstream.
interests: Japanese design and Wabi Sabi philosophy
goals: Create memorable interior spaces for commercial clients
Specify materials that meet fire class requirements
Build reputation for innovative material selection
Deliver projects on time and within budget
motivations: Standing out in competitive interior design market
Finding solutions that solve multiple client needs
Meeting strict building code requirements elegantly
Building long-term relationships with reliable suppliers
frustrations: Fire regulations that limit natural material options
Chemical fire retardants that compromise aesthetics
Materials that look great in samples but age poorly
behaviors: Attends design trade shows and material exhibitions
Maintains library of material samples and suppliers
Networks with other designers to share discoveries
Specifies materials with clear installation guidelines
preferredChannels: Design trade shows and material fairs
persona.quote: Fire regulations shouldn't mean sacrificing natural beauty. I need materials that meet code requirements while still creating the warm, authentic spaces my clients want.
persona.bio: Jan runs a boutique interior design studio specializing in hospitality and high-end commercial spaces. He's built his reputation on discovering unique materials that solve practical challenges while creating visually stunning environments. He's particularly interested in natural materials that meet fire safety requirements without relying on chemical treatments.
buyingTriggers: Commercial project requiring fire class B materials
Client request for unique, natural interior finishes
Need to differentiate from competitor projects
Restaurant, hotel, or retail interior project
Desire to create signature design element

persona.name: Marcus van der Berg, The Visionary Architect
persona.location: Amsterdam, Utrecht, or Rotterdam metropolitan areas
persona.occupation: Principal Architect or Design Director at boutique architecture firm
persona.personalityType: Marcus is detail-oriented and deeply committed to sustainable design principles. He views architecture as a bridge between tradition and innovation, constantly seeking materials that tell a story while meeting modern performance standards. He's respected in his field for creating buildings that age gracefully.
coreValues: Wabi Sabi philosophy of imperfect beauty
goals: Specify materials that meet strict fire safety regulations
Create distinctive facades that age beautifully
Achieve carbon-neutral or positive building certifications
motivations: Creating timeless architecture that improves with age
Finding unique materials that differentiate projects
Delivering exceptional value to discerning clients
Building a legacy of sustainable design
frustrations: Wood materials that fade, rot, or require constant maintenance
Fire safety regulations that force use of chemical treatments
Generic materials that make buildings look identical
Clients who don't understand the value of quality materials
Limited options for natural fire-resistant cladding
behaviors: Researches material certifications and lifecycle data thoroughly
Attends architecture exhibitions and material trade shows
Specifies materials early in the design process
Builds long-term relationships with specialty suppliers
Shares innovative material discoveries with professional network
persona.quote: I'm not just specifying cladding—I'm choosing how a building will tell its story over the next fifty years. The material has to be as thoughtful as the design.
persona.bio: Marcus leads a respected architecture firm specializing in sustainable residential and commercial projects across the Netherlands and Belgium. With over 15 years of experience, he's known for creating buildings that harmonize modern functionality with natural materials. He's particularly passionate about finding materials that embody the Wabi Sabi philosophy of beauty through imperfection and age.
buyingTriggers: Upcoming project requiring unique facade solution
Client request for sustainable, low-maintenance materials
Desire to create signature aesthetic for high-profile project
decisionCriteria: Fire safety compliance without toxic chemicals

product.name: Marugame Charred Accoya Wood
product.description: Exclusive charred Accoya wood featuring a characteristic silvery charred layer created using the traditional Japanese Shou Sugi Ban technique. Ideal for sustainable cladding for roofs, facades, and interiors with a guaranteed lifespan of at least 50 years. FSC certified with Cradle-to-Cradle gold certification.
product.pricingDetails: Pricing based on project specifications and volume
features: Accoya FSC certified wood from New Zealand
Traditional Shou Sugi Ban charring technique
Suitable for roofs, facades, and interiors
benefits: Natural fire resistance from charring process

product.name: Yoroi Charred Bamboo
product.description: Deeply charred bamboo with natural appearance and refined markings, specifically developed for situations requiring fire class B certification. Features natural fire retardant properties without added chemicals, making it ideal for commercial interiors and projects with strict fire safety requirements.
product.pricingDetails: Pricing based on project specifications and volume
benefits: Meets strict fire safety regulations naturally
useCases: Commercial interior walls requiring fire class B
Projects with strict fire safety codes

product.name: Zwarthout Professional Mounting Service
product.description: Expert assembly and installation service for charred wood products, ensuring correct installation without damaging the delicate carbon layer. Professional firemasters use specialized techniques and countersunk stainless steel screws with black heads for seamless, damage-free installation.
product.pricingDetails: Based on project drawings and specifications
features: Specialized techniques to protect carbon layer
Countersunk stainless steel screws with black heads
benefits: Prevents damage to delicate charred surface

competitor.name: Accoya
competitor.description: Sustainable modified wood manufacturer providing FSC-certified Accoya wood as base material for charred wood applications. Material supplier rather than direct competitor.
competitor.valueProposition: Durable, sustainable modified wood with extended lifespan
weaknesses: Raw material only, not finished product

competitor.name: MOSO
competitor.description: Bamboo products manufacturer and material partner. Supplies FSC-certified bamboo used in Yoroi charred bamboo products. Material supplier in adjacent category.
competitor.valueProposition: Sustainable bamboo materials for construction and design
weaknesses: Limited brand recognition in premium segment

competitor.name: Woodstoxx
competitor.description: Representative or distribution partner for Zwarthout products. Acts as intermediary rather than competitor, potentially handling sales or distribution channels.
